app.title: Serve Analysis
app.heroSubtitle: Upload a side-view clip of a single serve. Pose is detected on-device, the serve is split into phases, and rule-based checks flag movement errors.

controls.maxLowVisHelp: Higher — try to analyze even noisy clips.
controls.phaseSpeedHelp: Applied when a phase block is selected — click a block to replay it slowly.
controls.phaseHint: Click a phase to replay it slowly. Double-click the video to clear.

skeleton.errorHint: The skeleton is overlaid on the video (preview). Scrub the video to inspect detection: red dots mark joints where MediaPipe is unsure.

errors.video-too-long: Video is longer than 30 seconds. Upload a short clip of a single serve.
errors.serve-not-recognized: Could not recognize the serve. Shoot from the side, with the player fully in frame.
errors.pose-extraction-failed: Could not start pose recognition. Try a different browser/file.
errors.analysis-failed: Internal error while parsing poses. The skeleton is shown for diagnostics.
errors.video-read-failed: Could not read the video. Try a different file.

C3.title: Knee bend
C3.advice: Knees are barely bent — the legs contribute almost no energy to the hit. The legs are the engine of the serve: bending and pushing up is what launches you toward the ball. Bend the knees deeper in the "trophy" position to push upward toward the ball.
C3.adviceMild: The knees could bend a little more in the "trophy" position. A deeper knee bend lets the legs drive you upward toward the ball. Try to load the legs a touch more before the swing.

C1.title: Contact height
C1.advice: The hit happens too low — you strike with a bent arm. A low contact robs the serve of height and sends the ball into the net. Reach for the ball with a fully extended arm at the top of the stretch.
C1.adviceMild: The contact is a touch low — the arm isn't fully extended at the hit. Reaching a bit higher gives you more margin over the net. Try to extend the arm completely at the top of the stretch.

C2.title: Contact position
C2.advice: The strike point is well behind the body. Hitting behind you sends the ball into the net or makes it fly too long, and strains the shoulder. Toss and hit the ball slightly in front of you so the racket travels forward into the court.
C2.adviceMild: The strike point is slightly behind the body. Contact that far back costs power and accuracy. Aim to meet the ball just a little in front of you.

TO2.title: Toss height
TO2.advice: The toss is far too low — there isn't enough time for a full swing. A low toss forces a rushed, abbreviated motion and flattens the contact. Toss higher so the racket can make a full, relaxed circle.
TO2.adviceMild: The toss is a little low. A higher toss gives the racket time for a full swing and a higher contact. Try releasing the ball a touch higher.

TO1.title: Toss placement
TO1.metricName: Toss peak position relative to body
TO1.advice: The ball is tossed well behind the body. A toss behind you forces you to lean back at contact, killing power and sending the ball long. Toss slightly forward and to the side, into the path of the swing.
TO1.adviceMild: The toss drifts slightly back. A toss that lands in line with — or just ahead of — the body lets you hit through the ball. Try tossing a touch more forward.

T3.title: Toss arm
T3.metricName: Toss-arm height at contact (share of peak)
T3.advice: The toss arm drops well before contact. The raised toss arm is your pointer to the ball — dropping it early loses the reference and pulls the body down with it. Keep the toss arm up until the hit, then let it come down with the swing.
T3.adviceMild: The toss arm comes down a little early. Holding it up a beat longer keeps the ball in view and steadies the contact. Try leaving the toss arm up until just before the hit.

T1.title: Racket drop
T1.advice: The racket doesn't drop behind the back in the "trophy" position. That drop is what loads the swing — without it you push the racket forward instead of whipping it up. Relax the wrist and let the racket fall behind you before the throw.
T1.adviceMild: The racket drop is shallow in the "trophy" position. A deeper, relaxed drop builds more racket speed. Try letting the wrist loosen so the racket falls a bit further behind the back.

T2.title: Trophy pause
T2.advice: There is a long pause between the "trophy" position and the hit. A freeze there bleeds away the energy of the swing. Try to flow from the toss into the hit without stopping.
T2.adviceMild: The transition from "trophy" to the hit is a little slow. A smoother, continuous motion keeps the swing's momentum. Aim to start the throw the moment the trophy position is set.

F1.title: Follow-through
F1.metricName: Racket travel across the body after contact
F1.advice: The racket motion cuts off sharply after the hit. Stopping the swing short kills power and is hard on the shoulder. Finish the serve — let the racket travel across the body to the opposite side, decelerating naturally.
F1.adviceMild: The follow-through is a little short. A fuller finish across the body means more power and a healthier shoulder. Let the racket travel further across after the hit.

F2.title: Balance
F2.metricName: Body lean at the end of the serve
F2.advice: The body leans noticeably at the end of the serve. CV can't always tell a real loss of balance from a natural step into the court, so treat this as a hint: if it happens often, work on finishing the serve balanced over the front leg.

detect.too-few-frames: Only {{n}} frame(s) with a full skeleton were extracted — MediaPipe could barely find the figure (player too small/far, poor light, or heavy motion blur).
detect.low-visibility: Key joints are not visible on {{lowPct}}% of {{total}} frames (reject threshold {{maxPct}}%, visibility threshold {{visTh}}). Most often missing: {{worst}}. Usually the cause is framing (legs/knees cut off) or the player is too small.
